content.text: Derived haemodynamic parameters are calculated values that turn measured pressures, cardiac output, heart rate and body surface area into clinically useful patterns. In cardiothoracic ICU, these values help answer a more precise question than whether the blood pressure is high or low. They help identify whether the patient has poor forward flow, excessive afterload, inadequate preload, right ventricular failure, left ventricular failure, pulmonary vascular overload, vasoplegia, hypovolaemia, tamponade or mixed shock.

The key safety principle is that derived numbers are only as accurate as the measured inputs. Cardiac index is wrong if cardiac output is wrong. SVR is wrong if MAP, CVP or CO is wrong. PVR is wrong if mPAP, PAOP or CO is wrong. RVSWI and LVSWI are wrong if SVI, pressure gradients or transducer levels are wrong. Therefore, before acting on a calculated value, verify arterial line quality, transducer level, zeroing, PA catheter position, wedge validity, thermodilution technique, rhythm stability and clinical correlation.

content.definition: The total volume of blood ejected by the heart per minute. It is usually expressed in litres per minute and equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate.
Cardiac output corrected for body surface area. It is expressed in L/min/m² and allows comparison between patients of different size.
A calculated estimate of systemic afterload. It describes the resistance the left ventricle must overcome to generate forward systemic blood flow.
A calculated estimate of pulmonary afterload. It describes the resistance the right ventricle must overcome to eject blood through the pulmonary circulation.
An indexed estimate of the mechanical work performed by a ventricle per beat. LVSWI estimates left ventricular work and RVSWI estimates right ventricular work.

content.title: Core derived parameters at the bedside
rows: Primary systemic perfusion pressure target, but must be interpreted with flow and organ perfusion.
Most useful flow value for comparing patient size. CI less than 2.0 to 2.2 L/min/m² is concerning after cardiac surgery if perfusion is poor.
Volume ejected per beat. Low SV gives narrow pulse pressure and low flow.
Size-indexed stroke volume. Useful for calculating ventricular stroke work.
Systemic afterload. Low in vasoplegia, high in compensatory vasoconstriction or excessive pressor effect.
Systemic afterload indexed to body size.
Pulmonary afterload. High PVR increases RV work and can precipitate RV failure.
Usually interpreted in Wood units·m² or converted to dynes with ×80
Pulmonary afterload indexed to body size.
Right ventricular work per beat. Falling RVSWI with rising CVP suggests RV decompensation.
Left ventricular work per beat. Low LVSWI with high PAOP suggests LV failure with congestion.

content.text: The most useful CTICU use of derived variables is haemodynamic pattern recognition. Low MAP with low CI and high SVR suggests cardiogenic or hypovolaemic physiology, depending on filling pressures. Low MAP with normal or high CI and low SVR suggests vasoplegia. Low CI with high CVP, high PAP, high PVR and normal or low wedge suggests RV failure. Low CI with high PAOP suggests LV failure or left-sided congestion. Low CI with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure and equalising diastolic pressures suggests obstructive physiology such as tamponade.

Do not treat derived values as laboratory truths. They are calculations. A falsely low arterial MAP from an unlevelled transducer, an invalid wedge, an underdamped arterial line, an inaccurate cardiac output or a PA catheter in the wrong position can produce a completely misleading SVR, PVR, LVSWI or RVSWI.

content.title: Derived haemodynamic parameter map
content.description: A flowchart showing measured inputs on the left and calculated values on the right. Inputs include MAP, CVP, PA pressures, PAOP, CO, CI, HR and BSA. Outputs include SV, SVI, SVR, SVRI, PVR, PVRI, RVSWI and LVSWI.
elements: Measured arterial pressure generates MAP
Measured CVP contributes to SVR, SVRI and RVSWI
Measured PA pressures generate mPAP
Measured PAOP contributes to PVR, PVRI and LVSWI
Measured or calculated CO and CI generate flow-indexed variables
Heart rate converts CO or CI into SV or SVI
SVI and pressure gradients generate stroke work indices
teachingPoints: Derived numbers depend entirely on input quality.
SVR and PVR are pressure gradients divided by flow.
Stroke work indices combine flow per beat with the pressure the ventricle works against.

content.text: How to think about derived haemodynamic variables

Haemodynamic monitoring becomes powerful when pressure, flow and resistance are interpreted together. Blood pressure is not flow. A patient can have a normal MAP because SVR is high while cardiac output is critically low. A patient can have a low MAP because SVR is low while cardiac output is preserved. Cardiothoracic ICU patients commonly move between these states after bypass, bleeding, tamponade, arrhythmia, RV dysfunction, LV dysfunction, valve repair, vasoplegia and vasoactive therapy.

Derived parameters exist because no single measured value answers the whole question. MAP tells you the average systemic arterial pressure. CVP describes right atrial pressure, not volume. PAOP estimates left atrial pressure only if the wedge is valid. Cardiac output describes total flow, but without indexing it ignores body size. SVR and PVR estimate the resistance against which the ventricles must eject. RVSWI and LVSWI estimate the mechanical work of each ventricle per beat, combining stroke volume index with the relevant pressure gradient.

The calculation logic is simple. Flow equals pressure gradient divided by resistance. Rearranged, resistance equals pressure gradient divided by flow. Systemic vascular resistance uses the gradient from arterial pressure to right atrial pressure, divided by cardiac output. Pulmonary vascular resistance uses the gradient from mean pulmonary artery pressure to left atrial pressure or wedge pressure, divided by cardiac output. Stroke work indices use the stroke volume index multiplied by the pressure gradient across the relevant ventricle.
Measured inputs required before calculations

content.title: Measured and foundational inputs
rows: Invasive arterial line or cuff if no arterial line
Used to calculate MAP and interpret pulse pressure.
Arterial line mean pressure or calculated from SBP and DBP
Used in SVR, SVRI and LVSWI calculations.
Central venous catheter or PA catheter proximal port
Used in SVR, SVRI and RVSWI; indicates right atrial pressure, not true volume.
Used in PVR, PVRI and RVSWI; reflects RV afterload and pulmonary circulation pressure.
Used as left atrial pressure surrogate in PVR and LVSWI if wedge is valid.
Thermodilution, continuous PAC, echocardiography or other cardiac output system
Used to calculate CI, SV, SVR and PVR.
CO divided by BSA or continuous monitor output
Used for size-indexed flow and indexed resistance calculations.
Used to calculate stroke volume and stroke volume index.
Used to convert CO into CI and non-indexed values into indexed values.

content.text: Before calculating or acting, check whether each input is valid. MAP is unreliable if the arterial line is overdamped, underdamped, not zeroed or levelled incorrectly. CVP is misleading if the waveform is absent, the transducer is wrong, the catheter is malpositioned or intrathoracic pressure is high. PAOP is misleading if the wedge is over-wedged, under-wedged, measured at the wrong respiratory phase or invalid because of mitral disease, large v waves or pulmonary venous disease. CO is misleading if thermodilution technique is inconsistent, rhythm is irregular, tricuspid regurgitation is severe, shunt exists or injectate technique is poor.
Mean arterial pressure

MAP is the average arterial pressure across the cardiac cycle and the most practical systemic perfusion pressure. The common approximation at normal heart rates is DBP plus one third of pulse pressure, or DBP + 1/3 × (SBP − DBP). At high heart rates, diastole shortens and this approximation becomes less accurate, but monitors derive MAP by waveform integration when an arterial line is present.

In CTICU, MAP is usually targeted at least 65 mmHg for general organ perfusion, but the correct target depends on operation and patient physiology. A CABG patient with chronic hypertension, renal disease or graft flow concerns may need higher MAP, commonly 70 to 85 mmHg if ordered. A fresh aortic repair may require tighter systolic and MAP control to reduce shear stress. A cardiogenic shock patient needs enough MAP to perfuse coronary and systemic organs, but increasing SVR without improving cardiac output may worsen low-flow shock.
Cardiac output and cardiac index

Cardiac output is the total blood flow delivered by the heart per minute. It equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate. Normal adult CO is often around 4 to 8 L/min. Cardiac index divides CO by body surface area, giving a size-corrected value. Normal CI is commonly 2.5 to 4.0 L/min/m². In post-cardiac surgery patients, CI less than 2.0 to 2.2 L/min/m² is concerning when paired with low urine output, rising lactate, low SvO2, cool skin or escalating vasoactive support.

CI is often more clinically useful than CO because a CO of 4 L/min may be adequate for a small patient and inadequate for a large patient. However, CI still does not tell you why flow is low. Low CI with low CVP and low PAOP suggests low preload or hypovolaemia. Low CI with high PAOP suggests LV failure, mitral pathology or left-sided congestion. Low CI with high CVP, high PVR and low or normal PAOP suggests RV failure. Low CI with high CVP and equalising pressures suggests tamponade or obstructive shock.
Stroke volume and stroke volume index

Stroke volume is the volume ejected per beat. It is calculated as CO × 1000 ÷ HR. Normal adult SV is often 60 to 100 mL/beat. Stroke volume index is calculated as CI × 1000 ÷ HR and is usually around 33 to 47 mL/beat/m². These values help separate heart rate-driven output from actual beat strength. A tachycardic patient may have a deceptively preserved CO while SV is poor. A paced patient may have inadequate output if the rate is too low or if AV synchrony is lost.

In CTICU, SVI is especially useful when pulse pressure narrows. Low SVI can reflect hypovolaemia, tamponade, LV failure, RV failure, excessive afterload, severe valvular obstruction, myocardial stunning or loss of atrial contribution. If SVI improves after pacing optimisation, preload correction or afterload reduction, the intervention has improved mechanical output. If MAP improves only because SVR rises while SVI falls, organ flow may not improve.
Systemic vascular resistance and systemic vascular resistance index

SVR estimates systemic afterload. It is calculated as (MAP − CVP) ÷ CO × 80 and expressed as dynes·s·cm⁻⁵. The factor 80 converts Wood-unit style pressure-flow units into dynes·s·cm⁻⁵. A common adult SVR range is 800 to 1200 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵, though references vary slightly. SVRI uses CI instead of CO and is calculated as (MAP − CVP) ÷ CI × 80, with a common range around 1900 to 2400 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵·m².

Low SVR after cardiopulmonary bypass is common because inflammatory activation, vasodilatory mediators, rewarming, anaesthesia, acidosis, hypocalcaemia and vasoplegia reduce vascular tone. The typical pattern is low MAP, low diastolic pressure, wide pulse pressure, warm peripheries, low SVR and normal or high CI. The treatment emphasis is vascular tone restoration, adequate coronary perfusion pressure, correction of acidosis, calcium and temperature, and avoiding reflexive excessive fluid when flow is already adequate.

High SVR usually represents compensatory vasoconstriction or excessive vasopressor/afterload burden. In low cardiac output syndrome, the body raises SVR to defend MAP. This may preserve a number on the monitor while worsening LV ejection, increasing myocardial oxygen demand and reducing forward flow. In a failing LV, reducing excessive afterload can sometimes improve stroke volume and cardiac output, but this must be balanced against the need for adequate coronary and organ perfusion pressure.
Pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary vascular resistance index

PVR estimates pulmonary afterload. It is calculated as (mPAP − PAOP) ÷ CO and expressed in Wood units. Multiplying by 80 converts it to dynes·s·cm⁻⁵. A common adult PVR range is 1 to 3 Wood units. PVRI uses CI instead of CO and can be expressed in Wood units·m² or converted by multiplying by 80, depending on local monitor convention.

PVR is central to right ventricular assessment. The RV is thin-walled and highly afterload sensitive. Small increases in PVR can cause RV dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, septal shift, reduced LV filling and shock. Post-cardiac surgery triggers for increased PVR include hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, hypothermia, atelectasis, high airway pressure, high PEEP, pulmonary embolism, protamine reaction, pulmonary hypertension, mitral valve disease and LV failure.

Raised mPAP is not the same as raised PVR. If mPAP is high and PAOP is high, pulmonary pressure may be driven by left-sided congestion. If mPAP is high and PAOP is normal or low, PVR is more likely genuinely elevated. If both PAOP and PVR are high, the patient has combined post-capillary and pre-capillary physiology. This distinction matters because the treatment differs. LV failure with pulmonary venous hypertension needs left-sided unloading and LV optimisation. Pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension with RV failure needs RV perfusion support, PVR reduction and avoidance of hypoxia, hypercarbia and acidosis.
RVSWI

Right ventricular stroke work index estimates how much work the RV performs per beat indexed to body size. It is calculated as (mPAP − CVP) × SVI × 0.0136. A common range is about 5 to 10 g·m/m²/beat. The formula uses the pressure gradient across the RV during ejection and the indexed stroke volume. A high RVSWI may indicate the RV is working hard against pulmonary afterload. A low RVSWI can indicate poor RV contractile work, especially when paired with high CVP and low CI.

In RV failure, RVSWI may fall while CVP rises. This is an ominous pattern because the RV is no longer converting filling pressure into forward stroke work. The patient may show high CVP, high or rising PAP, elevated PVR, low CI, low SvO2, cool skin, rising lactate, oliguria, hepatic congestion and hypotension. Management priorities include maintaining RV coronary perfusion pressure, reducing PVR, correcting hypoxia, hypercarbia and acidosis, optimising rhythm and AV synchrony, avoiding excessive RV volume loading and escalating to echo and senior review.
LVSWI

Left ventricular stroke work index estimates how much work the LV performs per beat indexed to body size. It is calculated as (MAP − PAOP) × SVI × 0.0136. A common range is about 50 to 62 g·m/m²/beat. It combines indexed stroke volume with the systemic pressure gradient the LV ejects against. Low LVSWI with high PAOP suggests LV failure with congestion. Low LVSWI with low PAOP suggests underfilling, severe hypovolaemia, RV failure causing low LV preload, or globally poor contractility.

LVSWI is useful after CABG and valve surgery because it can reveal whether a MAP is being maintained by resistance rather than effective ventricular work. A patient with high SVR, low CI, low SVI and low LVSWI is not well perfused just because MAP is 70 mmHg. Conversely, a vasoplegic patient may have a low MAP but preserved CI and reasonable LVSWI. The treatment strategies are different.
Haemodynamic shock patterns

content.title: Typical derived-parameter patterns
rows: Surgical bleeding, vasodilation with underfilling, excessive diuresis or inadequate preload.
High initially, may vary with therapy
LV failure, graft ischaemia, myocardial stunning, severe mitral or aortic valve problem.
Post-bypass RV dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension, protamine reaction, PE, hypoxia, hypercarbia or high airway pressure.
Usually normal unless lung/RV disease coexists
Post-CPB vasodilatory shock, inflammatory vasoplegia, anaesthesia, acidosis or low calcium.
Mediastinal clot, pericardial tamponade, tension physiology or external cardiac compression.
Low, normal or high depending on phase
Common in CTICU: bleeding plus vasoplegia, LV failure plus vasoplegia, RV failure plus hypovolaemia, tamponade plus inotropes.

content.text: Mixed shock is common after cardiac surgery. A patient may bleed and become vasoplegic simultaneously. An LV failure patient may also have low SVR after bypass. An RV failure patient may be overfilled and vasodilated. A tamponade patient may have vasoactive-supported MAP that hides the severity of obstructed filling. Derived parameters help reveal the components, but only if the measured inputs are valid and interpreted against the patient.

content.title: Haemodynamic shock-pattern algorithm
content.description: An algorithm beginning with low MAP or poor perfusion, then separating low CI from preserved or high CI, then using CVP, PAOP, SVR and PVR to distinguish hypovolaemia, LV failure, RV failure, vasoplegia and tamponade.
elements: Start: low MAP, rising lactate, oliguria or poor perfusion
Decision 1: CI low versus CI normal or high
If CI normal or high with low SVR, label vasoplegia pattern
If CI low with low CVP and low PAOP, label hypovolaemia or bleeding pattern
If CI low with high PAOP, label LV failure or left-sided congestion pattern
If CI low with high CVP and high PVR, label RV failure pattern
If CI low with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure and equalising pressures, label tamponade or obstructive pattern
teachingPoints: Flow separates vasodilatory shock from low-output shock.
Filling pressure pattern separates left-sided, right-sided and preload problems.
Tamponade is a clinical emergency and should not wait for perfect numbers.

content.text: Pressure is not perfusion. MAP is the pressure available to drive flow, but organ blood flow depends on cardiac output, vascular resistance, microcirculation and oxygen content. A MAP of 70 mmHg can coexist with a cardiac index of 1.6 L/min/m², cool mottled skin and lactate 5 mmol/L. That patient is not adequately resuscitated simply because the pressure number looks acceptable.

SVR is often the missing piece after bypass. A post-CPB patient with MAP 55 mmHg may be bleeding, vasoplegic, tamponading or pump-failing. If CI is 3.6 L/min/m² and SVR is 520 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵, the dominant problem is vasoplegia. If CI is 1.7 L/min/m² and SVR is 1700 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵, the dominant problem is low flow with compensatory vasoconstriction. The treatment logic is opposite.

High CVP does not automatically mean adequate preload. It may mean RV failure, tamponade, high intrathoracic pressure, tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary hypertension, excessive PEEP or venous congestion. Giving fluid to a high-CVP, low-CI patient with high PVR may worsen RV dilation, septal shift and LV underfilling. The CVP must be interpreted with PAOP, PVR, RV function, echo and perfusion.

PAOP is not always LVEDP. Wedge pressure can approximate left atrial pressure and LV filling pressure only when the catheter is correctly wedged in a suitable lung zone and there is no major confounder. Mitral stenosis, mitral regurgitation with large v waves, atrial arrhythmias, high PEEP, pulmonary venous obstruction, over-wedging and poor waveform technique can mislead. An invalid wedge will corrupt PVR and LVSWI.

RV failure is often a pressure-flow mismatch. The CVP rises because the RV cannot empty effectively. The LV may be underfilled even when the venous side looks congested. The arterial waveform may show narrow pulse pressure. PA pressure may be high or may fall late when the RV fails completely. Falling RVSWI with rising CVP is particularly concerning because the ventricle is no longer converting filling pressure into forward work.

Vasoplegia can hide in plain sight. The patient may be warm with bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure, yet require high-dose noradrenaline to maintain MAP. If CI is preserved and SVR is low, repeated fluid boluses may not solve the problem and may worsen pulmonary oedema, haemodilution and right-heart strain. Correct acidosis, hypocalcaemia and hypothermia, and discuss vasopressin or other vasopressor strategy where appropriate.

LVSWI and RVSWI are more physiologically descriptive than many nurses realise. They help frame whether each ventricle is producing mechanical work. Low LVSWI with high PAOP points toward LV failure with congestion. Low RVSWI with high CVP points toward RV decompensation. These values should not be used in isolation, but they are excellent pattern-recognition tools when a PAC is already in place.

Indexing matters. A cardiac output of 4.0 L/min may be acceptable in a small adult and inadequate in a large adult. Cardiac index allows better comparison. The same principle applies to stroke volume index and resistance index. Indexed values are not perfect, but they prevent size-related misinterpretation.

Escalate urgently when derived variables indicate low flow with organ hypoperfusion: CI less than 2.0 to 2.2 L/min/m² with rising lactate, SvO2 less than 60 percent, urine output less than 0.5 mL/kg/hr, cool or mottled skin, new ECG changes, persistent MAP below target, high filling pressures, rising CVP, high PVR, narrowing pulse pressure or increasing vasoactive requirements.

The most useful interpretation sequence is: Is flow adequate? If not, is preload low, left-sided pressure high, right-sided pressure high, pulmonary resistance high, systemic resistance high or low, or is there obstructive physiology? Then ask whether the numbers are technically valid.
Where the calculated numbers come from

Derived haemodynamic variables are usually generated by a bedside monitor, haemodynamic module, PA catheter platform or cardiac output monitor. The monitor does not magically measure SVR, PVR, RVSWI or LVSWI. It calculates them from measured pressures, cardiac output, heart rate and patient demographic data. If the monitor has an incorrect height, weight or BSA, indexed values such as CI, SVI, SVRI and PVRI will be wrong. If the wrong pressure source is selected, the formula output will be wrong.

For a nurse-technician or senior bedside nurse, the equipment task is to verify the input chain. Arterial pressure requires a valid arterial waveform, correct damping, correct zero and level. CVP requires a valid central venous waveform and correct reference level. PA pressure requires correct distal PA waveform, not RV waveform and not persistent wedge. PAOP requires safe and valid wedge technique. CO requires reliable thermodilution or continuous cardiac output technique. Heart rate requires correct ECG detection, not double counting, pacing spike counting or artefact.

content.title: Pre-calculation reliability check
items: Confirm arterial waveform quality and dynamic response before trusting MAP for SVR or LVSWI calculations.
Confirm the arterial transducer is levelled to the right atrium and zeroed appropriately.
Confirm CVP waveform is present, appropriately scaled and levelled before using CVP in SVR, SVRI or RVSWI.
Confirm PA catheter distal waveform is genuinely pulmonary artery waveform, with PA diastolic pressure higher than RV diastolic pressure and a dicrotic notch visible where appropriate.
Confirm wedge pressure was obtained safely, briefly and at end-expiration, with return of PA waveform after balloon deflation.
Confirm cardiac output measurement technique is consistent, with acceptable repeated thermodilution values or stable continuous cardiac output conditions.
Check that height, weight and body surface area are entered correctly if the monitor calculates indexed values.
Review whether rhythm, severe TR, shunt, rapid infusion, fever, respiratory variation or ventilator dyssynchrony may corrupt cardiac output.

content.text: Monitor input errors that corrupt derived values

content.title: Input error to derived-value error
rows: Falsely high MAP may falsely elevate SVR and LVSWI, hiding hypotension or low perfusion.
Falsely low MAP may falsely lower SVR and LVSWI, causing unnecessary vasopressors or fluid.
MAP may be less affected than systolic, but severe damping affects pressure interpretation
May mimic low pulse pressure and low stroke volume or hide systolic hypertension.
SVR, SVRI, RVSWI and clinical preload interpretation
Falsely high CVP lowers calculated SVR and lowers RVSWI gradient; falsely low CVP does the opposite.
PVR, PVRI, LVSWI and left-sided filling pressure interpretation
Falsely high PAOP lowers calculated PVR and may exaggerate left congestion; falsely low PAOP raises calculated PVR.
CI, SV, SVI, SVR, PVR and stroke work values
SVR and PVR may appear falsely high, leading to wrong afterload interpretation.
CI, SV, SVI, SVR, PVR and stroke work values
SVR and PVR may appear falsely low, hiding vasoconstriction or pulmonary hypertension.
CI, SVI, SVRI, PVRI, LVSWI and RVSWI
Indexed values may overstate or understate severity in large or small patients.
Stroke volume may be falsely low or high if HR is double counted, undercounted or pacing spikes are counted.

content.text: Practical bedside display review

content.title: When a derived number looks wrong
items: Pause before treating the derived value itself. Ask which measured inputs created that number.
Check the waveform and value for each input: ART, CVP, PA, PAOP, CO, HR and BSA.
Repeat or verify the cardiac output if CI, SVR or PVR changes abruptly without clinical explanation.
Repeat wedge only if clinically necessary and safe; do not chase calculations by repeated wedging without indication.
Check whether recent interventions explain the change, including fluids, bleeding, pacing change, rhythm change, vasoactive dose change, ventilator change, suctioning, turning, warming or protamine.
Compare derived values with clinical markers: urine output, lactate, SvO2, skin, capillary refill, mentation, ECG, chest drains and echo.
Escalate when the derived pattern suggests shock, RV failure, LV failure, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or measurement error affecting treatment decisions.

content.text: PA catheter platform considerations

PA catheter systems may display continuous cardiac output, intermittent thermodilution values, SvO2, right-sided pressures and calculated variables. The nurse must know which values are live, which are averaged, which are manually entered and which are calculated. Continuous cardiac output values can lag behind sudden changes because they use averaging. Intermittent thermodilution values are more operator-dependent but can be repeated after a clinical change. Mixed venous saturation may change faster than averaged cardiac output and can warn of altered oxygen delivery.

When handing over a patient with PAC-derived values, include the waveform quality, catheter depth, last wedge validity, last CO method and variability, current rhythm, vasoactive doses, ventilator settings, temperature, lactate trend, urine output and whether derived values match the patient. A number without the conditions under which it was measured is unsafe.

content.title: Input validation before derived values
content.description: A checklist diagram showing that ART, CVP, PAOP, CO, HR and BSA must be validated before interpreting CI, SVR, PVR, LVSWI or RVSWI.
elements: Validate arterial MAP
Validate CVP
Validate PA pressure and mPAP
Validate PAOP or wedge
Validate cardiac output and cardiac index
Validate HR and BSA
Then interpret derived values
teachingPoints: Derived values are calculations, not independent measurements.
A wrong input can create a convincing but false haemodynamic diagnosis.
Equipment checks are clinical safety actions.

content.text: Normal and concerning ranges

content.title: Core haemodynamic values
rows: Less than 65 mmHg usually requires assessment and intervention unless an individual lower target is ordered and perfusion is adequate.
2 to 6 mmHg in many references; post-op ventilated patients often run higher
High CVP with low CI suggests RV failure, tamponade, tension physiology or severe congestion. Low CVP with low CI suggests underfilling or bleeding.
About 15 to 30 systolic and 4 to 12 diastolic mmHg
High PAP may reflect pulmonary hypertension, LV congestion, high PVR, hypoxia, hypercarbia or RV strain.
mPAP 20 mmHg or higher meets the modern haemodynamic threshold for pulmonary hypertension when assessed by right heart catheterisation, but ICU interpretation requires context.
Often 6 to 15 mmHg, reference ranges vary
High PAOP suggests left-sided filling pressure or congestion if valid. Low PAOP suggests low left-sided preload if valid.
Must be indexed for body size and interpreted with pressure, resistance and oxygen delivery.
Less than 2.0 to 2.2 L/min/m² with poor perfusion suggests low cardiac output syndrome or shock.
Low SV contributes to narrow pulse pressure and low flow.
Low SVI suggests weak beat output, underfilling, pump failure, high afterload or obstructive physiology.
Low SVR suggests vasoplegia. High SVR suggests compensatory vasoconstriction or excessive afterload.
Indexed systemic afterload; useful when patient size affects interpretation.
Greater than 3 Wood units suggests elevated pulmonary vascular resistance and increased RV afterload.
Calculated by Wood units × 80; high values increase RV failure risk.
Low with high CVP suggests RV decompensation. High may indicate RV working against high PVR.
Low with high PAOP suggests LV failure with congestion. Low with low PAOP suggests underfilling or poor contractility.
Less than 60 percent suggests impaired oxygen delivery, increased extraction, low cardiac output, anaemia, hypoxaemia or high metabolic demand.

content.text: Formula table

content.title: Formulae and units
rows: Wood units·m², or dynes·s·cm⁻⁵·m² if multiplied by 80

content.text: CTICU pattern targets

content.title: Procedure-specific interpretation
rows: MAP, diastolic pressure, CI, SVR, lactate, SvO2, urine output
Maintain MAP commonly at least 65 to 70 mmHg, protect coronary perfusion, identify low CI or excessive SVR early.
CI, PAOP, PAP, PVR, SVR, pulse pressure, rhythm
Relate numbers to corrected valve lesion. Watch for LV failure after aortic valve intervention, RV failure after mitral/tricuspid disease and pulmonary hypertension.
MAP, CI, pulse pressure, conduction rhythm, PAOP, vascular bleeding markers
Watch for conduction block, aortic regurgitation physiology, vascular bleeding and LV diastolic dysfunction.
Systolic pressure, MAP, CI, SVR, lactate, bleeding
Avoid excessive systolic pressure and shear while maintaining organ perfusion. Confirm arterial waveform accuracy before treatment.
CVP, PAP, mPAP, PAOP, PVR, CI, RVSWI, SvO2
Avoid hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and excessive PEEP. Maintain RV perfusion while reducing pulmonary afterload.
SVR, CI, MAP, diastolic pressure, lactate, temperature, calcium, acid-base status
Low SVR with preserved CI should prompt vasoplegia strategy rather than repeated fluid boluses.
CVP, PAOP, CI, pulse pressure, MAP, drain output, lactate
Do not wait for perfect calculations. Rising filling pressures, narrowing pulse pressure and low CI after cardiac surgery require urgent escalation.

content.text: Worked bedside examples

content.title: Pattern examples
rows: MAP 55, CI 3.5, CVP 8, PAOP 10, SVR 540, warm peripheries
Confirm line accuracy, support MAP and diastolic pressure, correct reversible vasodilatory contributors and avoid unnecessary fluid loading if flow is adequate.
MAP 62, CI 1.7, CVP 6, PAOP 7, SVR 1550, drain output 250 mL/hr
Bleeding or hypovolaemic low preload pattern
Escalate bleeding concern, check coagulation and haemoglobin trend, prepare blood products and surgical review.
MAP 60, CI 1.8, CVP 22, PAOP 9, mPAP 36, PVR 5 Wood units
RV failure or pulmonary hypertension pattern
Optimise oxygenation, ventilation, pH, rhythm and RV perfusion; avoid blind fluid loading and escalate for echo/senior review.
MAP 65, CI 1.6, CVP 12, PAOP 24, SVR 1500, pulmonary oedema
Escalate low-output LV failure, review inotropy, afterload, rhythm, ischaemia and echo.
MAP 58, CI 1.5, CVP 20, PAOP 20, pulse pressure 18, drain output suddenly stops
Tamponade or obstructive post-surgical compression until proven otherwise
Immediate senior and surgical escalation; prepare for urgent diagnostic echo and possible return to theatre.

content.text: A useful CTICU haemodynamic target is convergence: MAP at or above ordered target, CI usually above 2.2 to 2.5 L/min/m², lactate falling, urine output at least 0.5 mL/kg/hr, SvO2 usually above 60 to 65 percent, improving skin perfusion and no unexplained escalation in vasoactive support.

Escalate derived haemodynamic abnormalities immediately when they match patient deterioration: CI less than 2.0 to 2.2 L/min/m², SVR below 700 with hypotension, SVR above 1600 with low CI, PVR above 3 Wood units with RV dysfunction, PAOP above 18 to 20 mmHg with pulmonary oedema or low CI, CVP above 15 to 20 mmHg with low CI, RVSWI falling with rising CVP, LVSWI low with rising PAOP, or any pattern suggesting tamponade.

quiz.question: What is cardiac index?
options: Mean arterial pressure divided by heart rate
Cardiac output corrected for body surface area
Pulmonary artery pressure corrected for wedge pressure
Stroke volume multiplied by systemic vascular resistance
quiz.explanation: Cardiac index is cardiac output divided by body surface area. It is expressed in L/min/m² and allows flow comparison between patients of different size.

quiz.question: Which formula calculates systemic vascular resistance?
quiz.explanation: SVR is calculated from the systemic pressure gradient divided by flow: (MAP − CVP) ÷ CO × 80. It estimates systemic afterload.

quiz.question: A post-bypass patient has MAP 55 mmHg, CI 3.6 L/min/m², SVR 520 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵, warm peripheries and falling lactate. What is the dominant haemodynamic pattern?
quiz.explanation: Low MAP with preserved or high CI, low SVR and warm peripheries is typical of vasoplegia. Treatment focuses on restoring vascular tone and perfusion pressure while avoiding unnecessary fluid if flow is adequate.

quiz.question: A patient has CI 1.7 L/min/m², CVP 22 mmHg, PAOP 9 mmHg, mPAP 38 mmHg and PVR 5 Wood units. What pattern is most likely?
options: RV failure with raised pulmonary afterload
quiz.explanation: Low CI with high CVP, normal or low wedge, high mPAP and high PVR strongly suggests RV failure or pulmonary hypertension physiology. Blind fluid loading may worsen RV dilation and septal shift.

quiz.question: Which error would falsely lower the calculated PVR?
options: Falsely high PAOP from an invalid wedge
Using a lower body surface area
quiz.explanation: PVR equals (mPAP − PAOP) ÷ CO. If PAOP is falsely high, the pressure gradient becomes falsely small and PVR is falsely low. A falsely low CO or falsely high mPAP would falsely increase PVR.

quiz.question: A patient has MAP 70 mmHg, CI 1.6 L/min/m², SVR 1800 dynes·s·cm⁻⁵, cool mottled skin, SvO2 48 percent and lactate 5 mmol/L. What is the safest interpretation?
options: Perfusion is adequate because MAP is 70 mmHg.
The patient has low-flow shock despite preserved pressure.
The patient has pure vasoplegia because MAP is low-normal.
The derived values are irrelevant if the arterial line is present.
quiz.explanation: This is low-flow shock. High SVR may preserve MAP while cardiac output and oxygen delivery are inadequate. The clinical markers, low CI, low SvO2 and rising lactate override false reassurance from the MAP.

diagrams.title: Derived haemodynamic parameter map
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing measured inputs on the left and calculated values on the right. Inputs include MAP, CVP, PA pressures, PAOP, CO, CI, HR and BSA. Outputs include SV, SVI, SVR, SVRI, PVR, PVRI, RVSWI and LVSWI.
elements: Measured arterial pressure generates MAP
Measured CVP contributes to SVR, SVRI and RVSWI
Measured PA pressures generate mPAP
Measured PAOP contributes to PVR, PVRI and LVSWI
Measured or calculated CO and CI generate flow-indexed variables
Heart rate converts CO or CI into SV or SVI
SVI and pressure gradients generate stroke work indices
teachingPoints: Derived numbers depend entirely on input quality.
SVR and PVR are pressure gradients divided by flow.
Stroke work indices combine flow per beat with the pressure the ventricle works against.

diagrams.title: Haemodynamic shock-pattern algorithm
diagrams.description: An algorithm beginning with low MAP or poor perfusion, then separating low CI from preserved or high CI, then using CVP, PAOP, SVR and PVR to distinguish hypovolaemia, LV failure, RV failure, vasoplegia and tamponade.
elements: Start: low MAP, rising lactate, oliguria or poor perfusion
Decision 1: CI low versus CI normal or high
If CI normal or high with low SVR, label vasoplegia pattern
If CI low with low CVP and low PAOP, label hypovolaemia or bleeding pattern
If CI low with high PAOP, label LV failure or left-sided congestion pattern
If CI low with high CVP and high PVR, label RV failure pattern
If CI low with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure and equalising pressures, label tamponade or obstructive pattern
teachingPoints: Flow separates vasodilatory shock from low-output shock.
Filling pressure pattern separates left-sided, right-sided and preload problems.
Tamponade is a clinical emergency and should not wait for perfect numbers.

diagrams.title: Input validation before derived values
diagrams.description: A checklist diagram showing that ART, CVP, PAOP, CO, HR and BSA must be validated before interpreting CI, SVR, PVR, LVSWI or RVSWI.
elements: Validate arterial MAP
Validate CVP
Validate PA pressure and mPAP
Validate PAOP or wedge
Validate cardiac output and cardiac index
Validate HR and BSA
Then interpret derived values
teachingPoints: Derived values are calculations, not independent measurements.
A wrong input can create a convincing but false haemodynamic diagnosis.
Equipment checks are clinical safety actions.

references.title: Calculation of Pulmonary and Systemic Vascular Resistance
references.supports: MAP, SVR and PVR calculation principles, pressure gradients, flow-based resistance calculation and Wood unit conversion.

references.sourceName: Bootsma et al., Pulmonary artery catheter education review, PubMed Central
references.title: The contemporary pulmonary artery catheter. Part 2
references.supports: PAC-derived variables, normal ranges for CI, CO, CVP, SV, SVI, SvO2 and haemodynamic interpretation.

references.title: Derived values from cardiac output measurement devices
references.supports: Formulae for SVRI, PVRI, RVSWI, LVSWI and other derived values from cardiac output monitoring.

references.title: Pulmonary Artery Catheter
references.supports: Measured and derived PAC variables, normal ranges and clinical interpretation of PA catheter data.

references.title: Cardiopulmonary Monitoring of Shock
references.supports: Shock physiology, CVP interpretation, haemodynamic profiles and cardiopulmonary monitoring principles.

references.title: Invasive Hemodynamic Monitoring in Acute Heart Failure and Cardiogenic Shock
references.supports: Haemodynamic profiling using CI, SVR, PAWP and vascular patterns in acute heart failure and shock.

references.title: Pulmonary Hypertension in Adults
references.supports: Modern pulmonary hypertension threshold of mPAP at least 20 mmHg by right heart catheterisation and PVR-based interpretation.
